data.summary: Snowflake controls your content, can change rules, and forces arbitration.
data.savage_take: They own your content, control the rules, and if you object, good luck finding a real court.

red_flags.title: Mandatory Arbitration
red_flags.meaning: If you have a problem with the company, you can't sue them in a regular court; you have to go through a private process called arbitration.
red_flags.worst_case: If Snowflake wrongs you, you lose your right to a jury trial and must argue your case in a private setting, potentially with less favorable outcomes and limited appeal options.
red_flags.savage_explanation: Translation: Forget your day in court; any beef you have with them gets settled behind closed doors, on their terms, not yours. Your legal rights? Conveniently sidelined.

red_flags.title: Unilateral Changes to Terms
red_flags.meaning: The company can change these rules whenever they want, and if you keep using the service, you automatically agree to the new rules.
red_flags.worst_case: They could add new, unfavorable rules (like charging for a previously free service or further restricting your content rights) without needing your explicit consent, and your only option is to stop using the service.
red_flags.savage_explanation: Translation: They can change the rules mid-game and still act shocked when you complain, forcing you to play by their ever-shifting playbook.

red_flags.title: Broad License to Your Content
red_flags.meaning: When you upload your content, you give the company a permanent, worldwide right to use, copy, change, and share it for their business, even letting others use it.
red_flags.worst_case: Your creative work, apps, or data could be used by Snowflake or its partners in ways you didn't intend or approve, potentially for profit, without any payment or credit to you.
red_flags.savage_explanation: Translation: Your brilliant creations? They're now their property to play with, profit from, and sublicense to anyone, forever, for free. You just provide the content.
